current_fact.fact_text: On April 30, 1945, deep beneath a burning Berlin in the Führerbunker, Adolf Hitler and Eva Braun died by suicide less than two days after their marriage. Above them, Soviet troops hoisted the Victory Banner over the Reichstag, a stark symbol of the Nazi regime’s collapse.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

current_fact.match_reason: The end of a devastating era and the raising of a victory flag align with an episode about storms passing, new chapters, and cautious hope after darkness.

queue.fact_text: In 1961, the Soviet Union commissioned K-19, its first nuclear submarine armed with ballistic missiles. Nicknamed later the "Widowmaker" after a series of deadly accidents, it embodied both the technological ambitions and the human costs of the Cold War’s underwater arms race.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

queue.match_reason: A nuclear submarine in cold northern seas is mirrored by an episode that dives into Arctic waters, trading military tension for awe of marine life.

queue.fact_text: On April 30, 1963, citizens in Bristol launched a bus boycott against a company that refused to hire Black or Asian bus crews. Their sustained protest forced a policy reversal and became a catalyst for broader conversations about racial discrimination in the United Kingdom.

episode.title: Morning Drops #04 - Bars With Balls
episode.description: Yasmin dives into Women's Day through the voices of four incredible female rappers. Morning fire from the magic planet of Sedna FM — move your hands up and down with us. Hip hop culture through a feminine lens.
songs: Little Simz - Point and Kill (feat. Obongjayar)

queue.match_reason: A grassroots stand against discrimination fits with an episode centering powerful voices challenging injustice and demanding respect through hip-hop.

queue.fact_text: On April 30, 1973, the Watergate scandal deepened when President Richard Nixon fired his White House Counsel John Dean and accepted the resignations of top aides H. R. Haldeman and John Ehrlichman. These departures hinted at just how far the cover-up reached inside the Oval Office.

episode.title: Morning Drops #54 - Intentional Listening
episode.description:
Do you remember what it felt like to listen to music without a streaming platform?
In this Morning Drops episode, Gaia reflects on a recent experiment: stepping away from algorithmic listening and going back to an iPod. 

What starts as a small personal choice opens into a wider meditation on attention, presence, and what happens when music is no longer treated as something infinite, but as something we choose with care.

Tracklist:
Scientist - Drum Song Dub
The Other People Place - Let Me Be Me
A Forest Mighty Black - Fresh In My Mind
Cymande - Bra

queue.match_reason: A scandal about surveillance, secrecy, and tapes contrasts with an episode about listening with intention, inviting us to rethink how we pay attention to recorded sound and truth.

queue.fact_text: On April 30, 1975, North Vietnamese forces captured Saigon, prompting the last dramatic helicopter evacuations from the U.S. embassy rooftop. The city’s fall marked the official end of the Vietnam War and the beginning of a long, difficult process of reunification.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

queue.match_reason: A city and country moving into a new, uncertain chapter matches an episode about movement, travel, and the inner shifts that come with crossing borders.

queue.fact_text: In 1979, Mount Marapi on Sumatra erupted, sending ash and pyroclastic material into nearby communities and killing between 80 and 100 people. The volcano is a complex, restless system – part of the Pacific “Ring of Fire” that constantly reshapes Indonesia’s dramatic landscape.

episode.title: Morning Drops #46 - Fog and Fire
episode.description: In today’s Morning Drops, Gaia brings us on a passport-free trip between two Italian energies: the foggy stillness of the North and the volcanic pulse of the South. Throughout the journey, Gaia shares four of her favourite Italian songwriters. Grab your coffee and tune into the radio show from another planet!
songs: Edoardo Bennato - La Torre Di Babele
Lucio Dalla - Com'é Profondo il Mare

queue.match_reason: An explosive volcanic eruption fits the "fire" side of an episode that meditates on contrasting climates and the emotional weather they create.

queue.fact_text: On April 30, 1980, gunmen stormed the Iranian Embassy in London and took 26 hostages, sparking a tense, six-day siege. The drama ended when the SAS conducted a live-televised raid, an operation that would shape public perceptions of counterterrorism for decades.

episode.title: Morning Drops #32 - A Weekend In London
episode.description: Recorded in London, marking a weekend reunion between Gaia and Yasmin. A selection inspired by London's energy, seen through the eyes of two inseparable friends — music from concerts, club nights, and long walks across town. Four tracks to ease you into the week at the right pace.
songs: Jimmy Castor Bunch - It's Just Begun
The Asphodells - Late Flowering Lust (Phil Kieran Remix)

queue.match_reason: A historic high-stakes moment in London is paired with an episode steeped in the city’s contemporary pulse, connecting past tensions with today’s creative energy.

queue.fact_text: On April 30, 1982, the Bijon Setu massacre in Calcutta saw a mob brutally attack and kill 17 members of the Ananda Marga order, including monks and a nun, on a bridge at dawn. The incident remains a haunting example of how rumor and prejudice can suddenly ignite into collective violence.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

queue.match_reason: A dark episode of human violence is juxtaposed with a genre known for introspective, cinematic moods, inviting quiet reflection rather than spectacle.

queue.fact_text: In 1993, CERN announced that the World Wide Web’s core protocols would be made freely available to anyone. That single decision opened the doors for an open, global web – turning a physics lab tool into the infrastructure for today’s digital culture.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

queue.match_reason: The choice to make the web free reshaped how we connect, echoing an episode that asks us to examine our digital dependence and relationship with always-on connectivity.

queue.fact_text: On April 30, 1999, neo-Nazi David Copeland detonated his third and final nail bomb at the Admiral Duncan pub, a gay bar in Soho, London. The attack killed three people and injured dozens, targeting a space that had long served as a refuge and community hub for LGBTQ+ people.

episode.title: Morning Drops #51 - The Dancefloor
episode.description:
For International Women’s Day, this Morning Drop turns its focus to women on the dancefloor, not as a trend, but as a cultural force.

Across five tracks, Gaia follows a line from disco closers to hip hop club classics, tracing how the dancefloor became a place where women could take space, build confidence, and create connection, sometimes with friends, sometimes alone, always with music at the centre.

This episode isn’t about pointing fingers. It’s a gentle reminder that spaces like the dancefloor still matter: places where people can express themselves, meet each other with respect, and feel part of something bigger than a night out.

Tracklist:
Donna Summer - Last Dance (1978)
The Sequence  - Funk you up
Salt-N-Pepa - Push It
Queen Latifah - Ladies First
Alicia Bridges - I Love the Nightlife

queue.match_reason: An attack on an LGBTQ+ pub strikes at the heart of nightlife as sanctuary, resonating with an episode about the dancefloor as a vital, empowering social space.

queue.fact_text: On April 30, 2008, scientists in Russia confirmed that two sets of skeletal remains found near Yekaterinburg belonged to Alexei and Anastasia, children of Tsar Nicholas II. The discovery helped close a 90-year-long historical mystery filled with rumors, impostors, and unanswered questions about the fate of the Romanov family.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

queue.match_reason: A long-delayed closure to a tragic family story aligns with an episode about healing over time, looking back to understand and finally move forward.

queue.fact_text: On April 30, 1900, the United States formally made Hawaii a U.S. territory, with Sanford B. Dole installed as its first governor. This legal step followed the overthrow of the Hawaiian monarchy and paved the way for Hawaii’s eventual statehood, forever altering its political and cultural landscape.

episode.title: Morning Drops #21 - Endless Summer
episode.description: Recorded from Fano, a quiet town on the Adriatic coast where summer stretches long and slow. Between swims, simple meals, and time with family, Gaia reflects on the kind of music that helps us return to ourselves. Through different shades of jazz — from minimal and meditative to warm and rhythmic — an invitation to slip into that endless summer feeling.

queue.match_reason: Hawaii’s transformation into a U.S. territory evokes images of sun, sea, and complex histories, paired with an episode steeped in coastal, endless-summer atmospheres.

queue.fact_text: On April 30, 1492, the Spanish Crown granted Christopher Columbus his commission of exploration, naming him admiral of the ocean sea and viceroy of any lands he might claim. The document set in motion voyages that would radically reshape maps, ecosystems, and societies across the Atlantic world.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

queue.match_reason: A royal commission that launches ocean voyages connects with an episode about travel, landscapes, and the seafaring song "Ma come fanno i marinai".

queue.fact_text: In 1598, Juan de Oñate set out to conquer Santa Fe de Nuevo México, leading Spanish settlers and soldiers north from New Spain. His expedition founded new colonies but also brought brutal repression and long-lasting scars to Indigenous communities in the region.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

queue.match_reason: A colonial expedition into arid lands parallels an episode about desert peoples, resistance, and music born from survival in harsh frontiers.

queue.fact_text: On April 30, 1513, Edmund de la Pole, a Yorkist claimant to the English throne, was executed on orders of King Henry VIII. His death symbolically closed one of the lingering chapters of dynastic rivalry that shadowed the early Tudor reign.

episode.title: Morning Drops #36 - Women of Rock
episode.description: A tribute to the women who shaped the sound of rock, the ones who picked up a guitar and refused to ask for permission. The conversation unfolds in Italian, led by Gaia and her father. A dialogue between generations and a reminder that the pulse of rock has always belonged to anyone brave enough to play it loud. Distortion, rhythm, freedom.
songs: Joan Jett & The Blackhearts - I Love Rock 'n Roll

queue.match_reason: A story of royal power struggles and rigid hierarchies is contrasted with loud, boundary-breaking rock – music that symbolically cuts through inherited authority.

queue.fact_text: In 1305, Roger de Flor, the charismatic leader of the mercenary Catalan Company, was assassinated in Adrianople after entering Byzantine service. His murder unleashed a wave of retribution by his hardened troops, who ravaged Thrace in what became known as the Catalan Vengeance.

episode.title: Morning Drops #10 - The One With Many Names
episode.description: Yasmin guides us through the glamorous world of Ghost, the Swedish band that turned rock into ritual. From masked clergy to shifting identities, diving into the music and myth of one of the most theatrical acts of our time.

queue.match_reason: Mercenary armies, betrayal, and vengeance carry an almost operatic darkness that fits the theatrical, ritual-infused world of Ghost.

queue.fact_text: On April 30, 1982, a quiet bridge in Calcutta became the site of the Bijon Setu massacre, when a mob lynched members of a spiritual order. The tragedy exposed how quickly fear and misinformation can turn ordinary public spaces into scenes of horror.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

queue.match_reason: An event marked by sudden, chaotic violence is paired with an episode dedicated to slowing down and breathing, offering a sonic counterweight of calm and reflection.

queue.fact_text: On April 30, 1973, when the Watergate scandal claimed John Dean and other top aides, it set the stage for Dean’s later testimony, in which he famously described a "cancer on the presidency." His transformation from loyal counsel to key witness became a defining arc in the story of presidential accountability.

episode.title: Morning Drops #52- Think Again
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the quiet art of changing your mind, guided by Adam Grant's book Think Again. From the beliefs we carry without questioning to the identities we build around what we think we know, this episode follows the slow, sometimes uncomfortable process of unlearning.

Along the way, we meet a Serbian trumpet player who had to let go of one dream to find another, a Tunisian oud master who challenged centuries of tradition from the inside, and a Japanese band who found freedom in playing for no one. The music moves from Balkan brass to Mediterranean stillness to psychedelic drift, tracing the shape of a mind opening up.

Listen slowly, and ask yourself: is there something you believe today that deserves a second look?

Tracklist:
Boban Marković Orkestar – SAT
Anouar Brahem – The Astounding Eyes of Rita
Kikagaku Moyo – Dripping Sun
Son Lux – Lost It to Trying

queue.match_reason: John Dean’s shift from defender to whistleblower is a real-world example of rethinking deeply held loyalties, mirroring an episode devoted to the courage of changing your mind.

queue.fact_text: On April 30, 1961, the nuclear submarine K-19 entered service as a symbol of Soviet technological pride; just a year later, a reactor leak forced its crew to improvise life-saving repairs with minimal protection. Many sailors absorbed lethal doses of radiation, turning a secret mission into a tragic story of sacrifice.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

queue.match_reason: A story of going literally deep below the surface in a submarine connects with an episode about immersion, water, and what it means to dive fully into an experience.

queue.fact_text: On April 30, 1963, the Bristol Bus Boycott showed how a local action could ripple nationally: a few dozen activists, inspired by the U.S. civil rights movement, stood firm until a bus company dropped its discriminatory “colour bar.” Their victory helped pave the way for Britain’s first Race Relations Act.

episode.title: Morning Drops #08 - (un)Boxing
episode.description: An episode about breaking free from expectations and labels. Music that helps us unbox ourselves and embrace who we truly are.
songs: Kendrick Lamar - The Blacker the Berry

queue.match_reason: A boycott that challenged racial ‘colour bars’ pairs with an episode explicitly about breaking boxes, labels, and limiting expectations imposed on people.

published.fact_text: In 1897, J. J. Thomson stood before the Royal Institution in London and announced that atoms were not indivisible after all. He had found the electron – a tiny, negatively charged particle more than 1,800 times lighter than a proton – cracking open the door to modern physics and electronics.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

published.match_reason: A breakthrough in understanding the smallest building blocks of matter pairs with an episode about how learning reshapes the brain and the joy of discovery.

published.fact_text: On April 30, 1905, Albert Einstein quietly completed his doctoral thesis at the University of Zurich. Within months, this modest academic milestone would be followed by his "annus mirabilis" papers, reshaping our understanding of space, time, and light.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: Einstein’s intense, device-free thinking contrasts with our hyperconnected era, echoing an episode about disconnecting from screens and giving the mind space to wander.

published.fact_text: In 1939, NBC launched its first regular television service from New York City by broadcasting Franklin D. Roosevelt’s opening address at the World’s Fair. For many viewers, this wasn’t just a speech – it was the first time moving images and voices arrived together, live, in their living rooms.

episode.title: Evening Flows #00 - Introduction to evening flows
episode.description:
Sedna FM introduces a new format: Evening Flow, where you can enjoy curated music sets from selectors, DJs, and artists.

Each mix is an invitation to drift beyond the expected. A space where hidden gems from personal archives come to light, where sound speaks its own language. There are no rules here, no patterns to follow. Just the steady unfolding of a mood, a texture, a journey shaped by feeling.

Let the music lead the way - enjoy the trip!

published.match_reason: The birth of regular TV broadcasting fits an episode introducing a new broadcast format, both marking fresh frontiers in how stories and sound reach people.

published.fact_text: On April 30, 1943, the British submarine HMS Seraph surfaced off the coast of Spain to release a single corpse into the sea. Dressed as a courier and carrying forged documents, this man – part of the covert Operation Mincemeat – would successfully mislead Nazi planners about the Allied invasion of Europe.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: A clandestine wartime deception aimed at undermining a regime resonates with an episode about political resistance and challenging entrenched power.

published.fact_text: On April 30, 1945, deep beneath a burning Berlin in the Führerbunker, Adolf Hitler and Eva Braun died by suicide less than two days after their marriage. Above them, Soviet troops hoisted the Victory Banner over the Reichstag, a stark symbol of the Nazi regime’s collapse.
